topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the Natural Hazards Commission and 2024/25 Annual review of Southern Response Earthquake Services Limited
description: Wednesday 11 February 2026| Finance and Expenditure Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the Natural Hazards Commission and 2024/25 Annual review of Southern Response Earthquake Services Limited Southern Response Earthquake Services Limited Casey Hurren, Chief Executive Bevan Killick, Chair Natural Hazards Commission Chris Black, Board Chair Tina Mitchell, Chief Executive Chris Chainey, Chief Financial Officer Michala Beacham, Chief Strategy Officer
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the Natural Hazards Commission and 2024/25 Annual review of Southern Response Earthquake Services Limited. Southern Response Earthquake Services Limited. Casey Hurren, Chief Executive

chunks.text: Still waiting for

The table when you're ready. Uh, yeah, yeah. Um, You want some response first? No, no, no, no. We're just waiting on natural hazards. Oh, okay. Okay. Okay. We'll get them. We did say 18, so we'll give them, yeah. Hi. Thanks, Tara.
Go. We appreciate your response in the, in the circumstance.

Okay. We're welcome. The chief executive, the chair. Thank you for being here ahead of time. And, uh, we've got 15 minutes allocated to that if we need that. So we're welcoming the introductory remarks. Thank you. Well, Morena, I'm Bevin Killick, um, the Chair of Southern Response. So Southern Response is a crown company with a limited purpose that is to settle the Canterbury earthquake claims from the failed policy holder, owned mutual insurer, a MI, the Canterbury Earthquake claim claims are considered by globe, the global insurance industry to be some of the

most complex ever. This is due to most of the policies being total replacement, cover apportionment between earthquake events, apportionment between what was BQC now, natural Hazards Commission and insurers changing building regulations. And in some cases, commissioners put into building regulation departments of councils, Various local bodies being involved, uninsured properties, underinsured properties, multi-unit builds with different insurers under insurance, no insurance.

And I could go on. So the previous chair, Alistair James, retired at the end of June, 2025. He was not replaced. And as we run down, we are now a board of three. I've been appointed chair our Dean, Boto Corkin, now chair, the Audit and Risk Committee, and Fiona PIM is the third director in the last year.

We have largely concluded the preoc October, 2020, uh, sorry, 2014 payment package. This resulted from a class action from customers who had settled prior to October, 2014, as opposed to those who had opted for a managed repair or rebuild, and who the higher courts determined were entitled to additional payments.

Upon conclusion of the project, the associated staff were made redundant. This project was significantly more successful than we had expected, as it was difficult to track down people and convinced them that they were entitled to additional funds. In terms of business as usual, we have now settled over 50,000 claims and over 900, 9,300 over cap claims.

We have been directed to work closely with the Natural Hazards Commission to resolve the remaining claims. The insurance industry knows claims do not get better with time. We have some strategies to bring claims to the fore earlier. Um, I have our chief executive, Casey Huran with me today, and we are happy to take any questions. Okay, Thank you. I'm gonna go to, um, Dr. Deborah Russell.

Thank you. Nice to see you, Casey. Um, um, the first set of things, I mean, um, making people redundant, how many staff were made redundant? 13. 13. And what were you able to do to help 'em find new employment? Uh, so we had, uh, we had, um, these sessions run with, um, people that specialized in creating CVS and things like that.

Um, we also were pretty open about the fact that redundancies were happening, so if there's anything we could do to facilitate 'em into, um, new roles, we would. And I'm pleased to say that every one of those, um, people have found a new job. So Great. So there's a really good process there. Yeah, It was, and we had no sort of pushback on it. Everybody knew it was a limited life sort of project. Yeah.

So it was, um, it was one of those things where everybody knew what they'd signed up for and yeah, we did what we could to facilitate 'em into those new roles. And of course, Christchurch is the, um, booming and happening place to be at the moment. So It's great. It's a good place to be at the moment, so, yeah. Yeah. Okay. So that sounds really good. Um, look, this is an impossible question to answer, so I'm gonna ask it anyway. How long, How long? Um, it's, and that's one of the things that I think that we've been working on at the moment, is what can we do

to expedite the claims? Um, one of the interesting things that we've discovered at the moment is that the average age of our policy holders now is, um, about 65. And so what we're also seeing is that two thirds of our policy holders are the original homeowners. So what you'd expect in the next sort of 10 to 20 years is that that group would look to sell their house and then may discover that there are some issues with the original repairs.

So it still could go on for quite a long time as people move on and buy and sell houses in Canterbury. 'cause that's, as I've said to you previously, is that that's often the catalyst for discovering that there's a problem is the buying and selling of property. Um, so what we are trying to do at the moment is, is there a way that we can be more sensible about trying to track down those people and potentially go to their properties and say, we just need to check to see that it was done properly the first time.

And that's the thing we're doing at the moment, but it's a, we don't want to, um, spook people or scare people unnecessarily. So we are looking at, um, smarter ways of doing it, like around land conditions. So if it was all land at the time or if there was, um, certain characteristics of the property, so was it a heavier property, um, with heavier cladding, that might mean there was more damage as well.

And so we are doing a lot of that desktop work at the moment to try and assess that. And for us to be able to do that, NH C's being very cooperative in terms of supplying data. So it's, um, we want to do it, as I say, in a sensible way, not a way where we are haphazard in unnecessarily alarming people. So in answer to your question, if we sat on our hands and did nothing, it would be another 20 or 30 years we could potentially be getting claims, um, just as that, that demographic ages and moves on into, um, downsizing

or into, um, retirement homes. Um, if we can do the work that we're doing at the moment, well then we're hoping we can bring that forward. Yeah. So it's, it's a long process. Yes. Um, trying to recall, when I was minister, I think you told me that after the LA earthquake or in 20 No, 19 something or Other, nine five.

Nine five is Yes. Something in the nineties. Yeah. It Took about 25 years. Yes. Yep. And is it, yeah, and this is even more complicated than that because you've got multiple earthquakes you've had, um, and having the two layers of insurance, so NHC initially Yeah. And then us coming in. So for these claims that I'm talking about, it's, these aren't, um, claims that we've been dealing with.

These are new ones yet that even come in the door. So these are the ones that we yet to, um, deal with. And that's why for us, there isn't the opportunity for us to just say, sorry, we're, we're packing up and closing down now. Because for some of these people, they're gonna discover this problem as they're dealing with their largest asset thinking that, you know, they've worked hard all their lives and then all of a sudden discover that there might be a problem.

So that's, that's, that's a difficulty more than this. Okay. Okay. Thanks. And if, if, if I may, the, um, the LA earthquake claims would've been probably an indemnity policy rather than a total replacement replacement. Oh, yeah. Yeah. So yeah. And total replacement up to the current code, there's a lot of additional complexity and in, in these ones, so Yeah.

Yeah. Dr. David Wilson. Um, first of all, thank you for your work. Um, in some cases it's a very thankless task and, um, the government has stepped in perhaps in places where private sector may have had a better response. Um, so I'm actually interested in your views on how insurers are reacting.

Now. I'm hearing from constituents that perhaps there's a little bit of over egging the risks associated with climate change and with these natural hazards, and it's generally moving out beyond those areas that you might say are, are high risk. Yeah. Um, I, I hear what you're saying in, in some, in some ways it's sort of slightly out of scope for Yeah.

Um, sounds like a very insurancey term to, um, you know, it's, you know, we are looking back in terms of the policies that were in place. So, um, if I can caveat sort of Yeah. My, my answer by saying Yeah, that's not our concern. Yeah. Um, with due respect. Yeah. Um, but, um, you know, I, I think New Zealand has always had great penetration of insurance, and we've been very lucky for that.

And, and that's evident in the way Christchurch looks now. Um, that's something that's unusual globally and, um, I, I think, you know, these insurance companies are corporate entities, um, that, um, are there to make a dollar for their shareholders.

Um, and, you know, we wouldn't in New Zealand enjoy the insurance penetration that we have if it wasn't for schemes like the Natural Hazards Commission. Mm-hmm. And, um, if, if you go to Lloyd's of London where I used to work, um, that is a scheme that is admired internationally.

Um, you know, nothing's gonna be perfect, but if it wasn't for that, we wouldn't have the, the penetration of insurance that we do in New Zealand. So, um, these commercial entities will be looking at, um, where, um, they are overexposed and, um, sometimes it, it, I I don't think that the insurance industry is doing a great job at explaining why they are hesitant at some areas.

Yeah. I mean, would end, it was recently in the, in media and I, I, I feel that is an unusual, um, place to, um, Westport. I get, I mean, there's some real flood risk there. Um, you know, maybe some places need to be moved. Um, what ends unusual, I think it's actually the, the capacity of those insurers to, for a geographic lo location, they're overexposed to that location.

Um, it's actually probably no different to Ash Burton or Nui or anywhere else. Um, so there's some sort of things in the background that I don't think they're explaining very well. Um, they are going to price for risk. Yeah. Um, Already are, Well, well, they're, they're, and, and that's because, um, but you know, we, we need a, a, a, a really broad approach as a, as a country to this, which includes where councils are consenting, properties are being built.

Um, so it, it, it is a quite a wicked problem. Um, and, and they will price for that. But, um, I think, you know, we need to look more broadly as to how we tackle it. Yeah. Given your experience looking to the future, there's, there's a real role here. True. Yeah. Okay. Anyone else? Yeah, yeah.

Just, um, just a quick question, uh, more in a gent. Um, I, I, I was just reading through the board charter and I didn't see any kind of treaty clause or, um, any obligation to that. So just part of my question is, is how, how have you been working with some of the Maori communities down there and, and across the country in some of those particular areas?

Uh, and I would suspect that many in the no insurance space, um, and especially when you head up to, you start heading into, um, and places like that where the, um, uh, we, we saw that with the, the floods. Um, but just wanting to know in terms of the, the work that you do, um, how, how closely do you work with kind of the Maori entities iwi in regards to, um, the may you do with, uh, Southern Response? Sure.

So I think, um, first, you know, these are all Canterbury Earthquake claims, um, from the, the 2010 11 earthquake sequence. Um, so we, um, uh, nhu, uh, a big in our, um, um, in, in our mindset, uh, Fiona Pim is Nhu, uh, who's so a, a third of our border, um, from the local iwi, if you like.

Um, we've, we've worked closely, um, particularly with the, uh, you know, when we've opened buildings and had them blessed and, um, as a board, um, done our own, um, cultural awareness and tore training. Um, and, um, we've found, I, I don't think the local Maori population have been, um, and we have looked at this quite deeply, um, have been, um, materially differently treated or, or, uh, in different situations to, to the, the, um, to everyone else.

Um, we, we have been sensitive to that. Um, uh, but no, we've, we've had great engagement with, in fact, we were over the road from them, in fact, next door at one stage, um, and in show place where the, um, the nu is, um, down and in, um, tower Junction.

So, um, yeah, I, I, is there much more you'd add to that? I, I think one of the challenges is that where you mentioned no insurance, and one of the things that for us to actually, um, settle the claim in the first place, they have to have an insurance policy in place. And so one of the, the things that I guess we had an engaged with Maori in that way was that a number of people that don't have insurance simply wouldn't get to us.

And so, um, I guess any opportunities to deal with people in more fraught circumstances, like those ones without insurance just never came in our way. And that's, that's, that's the, the, I guess the, the simple answer and all of that is that, um, expectancy we're conscious of, um, how you engage with Maori and, um, and what we should be doing in those circumstances.

But the, the demographic of people that were our claimants, um, proportionately, they just weren't a large number of Maori that were there. Thank you. Thank you. Any, any further questions? No. Okay. Thank you, gentlemen. Thank you. Appreciate, Appreciate All work you've done on your written questions and answers too much. Well done. Thank you. Just, okay.
We've got the Natural Hazards Commission outside.
They're not going, they're wanting to spend more time on the road in the election, so, mm. Good morning. Yeah.

Firstly, apologies. We, uh, didn't realize we were tied by the list that were shut off today. So not, not the first time Yeah. Happened to all of us. Oh, right. Okay. Well, we don't feel quite so bad there. Thank you. Yeah, no, that's all right. No welcome. Um, uh, we look forward to some introductory remarks and some introduction of your, uh, uh, team.

And then we'll, um, do a bit of a q and a. We've got 45 minutes allocated, see how we get. Thank you. Okay. Thanks Chair. Uh, just introduce, uh, um, I've gotta start with Tina, Tina Mitchell, who's our chief executive. Oh, good. Uh, we've got Chris in the back here as our CFO, Chris Cheney and Mikala Ham was our, uh, head of strategy.

And thanks for the opportunity to, uh, share with you some of the progress we've made, some of the achievements, some of the challenges, and some of the opportunities. Um, firstly just acknowledge the recent weather events that's been very tough, uh, on those affected, obviously. Um, happy to talk more about that if you'd like us to. I think one of the, I've been, uh, in this role as chair for three, just three plus years now.

And one of the things that I was very impressed with coming into the role was the very clear strategic framework that Natural Hazard Commission Akata has. Um, and it's around, uh, upstream, downstream, so there's resilience, uh, there's readiness, there's recovery, and there's risk financing supporting all of that. We haven't changed that for five years, actually.

It's a very sound, uh, logical and workable framework in terms of, uh, the overall message, um, for the committee. Look, I think we've had a really good year. Um, good progress. We've hit 37 out of 39 of our, uh, SOPE measures. Um, so good performance. A couple of there we've missed. One is around the short dated, uh, settlement of claims, uh, just 3% short of what we expected there, and it's been tough.

The other one is, uh, it's been tough on the, uh, satisfaction, um, for about 57 homeowners, uh, the residual 57 homeowners that we have with, uh, the natural, uh, the, uh, north Island weather events. Uh, and that's primarily to do with complexity around land cover, and happy to talk more about that. But overall, very, uh, pleasing.

Uh, in terms of, uh, the financial side, uh, you know, we, we posted a, um, good surplus this year, um, close to 300 million. That's very helpful when you've got negative equity of 700 million. So it takes us back closer to zero, uh, but not quite. Um, and of course that's reflective, um, of a benign, uh, claims here last year, about 2000 claims.

So that's very low, uh, for us, and we are ready for the sort of big event that comes next on the cost side. Look, we are very disciplined about our cost. We always have been. Uh, we continue to be, uh, we came in under budget, uh, 3% less year on year, I'm sure on the restatement. Um, I'm sure the auditors have taken you through that on behalf of the audit general, but happy to, um, uh, take any questions on that.

Um, there's no economic impact on that, but, but it is a restatement and just acknowledge that. Finally, organizational health, um, how's, uh, you know, the Natural Hazard Commission, uh, traveling in terms of the organization? Uh, well, we think, uh, we've got a very good, um, team. We've got good engagement, and this last year our public, uh, reputation in X was 62, which is the highest it's ever been.

So that was very pleased. Thank you. Okay, thank you. We're gonna go to Deborah Russell, Dr. Deborah Russell. Lovely to see you. Very nice to see you. Yeah. Um, so much to talk about. So, um, let's, uh, I just wanna start with the, um, levy, um, at I, through last year, there was, um, a discussion as to whether or not the, um, natural Hazards Commission levy would be increased or not.

And I know that was a very live discussion, but just in the last few weeks we've heard that it's not going to be increased for the time being. Yes. What impact is that going to have on how you structure your Yeah, so our levy, as you know, has been 16 cents per a hundred dollars. Um, in terms of actuarial modeling around breakeven, it's not sufficient.

Um, so it does need to go up. In some states there's a question about timing on that. Um, and of course, everyone pays the same levy. So when that's a decision for the government, um, of the day. Um, and at the moment, I think the priority seems to be on the review of the private sector, uh, insurance. Uh, and there's a review going on with that at the moment.

So I imagine it will come, uh, back onto the table after that, but it definitely does need to go up. So it needs to go up in terms of the cents per dollar of cover. Yep. Yes, it does. That's right. Yep. Um, yeah, so on, on a, uh, actuarial basis, um, you know, we can handle the, you know, attritional losses as we call them, you know, the current weather events.

So if you look at the recent weather events for us, you know, that'll be less than a thousand funds. If you look at the North Island weather events, that's 11,000 claims. And if you look at Canterbury, that's 170,000 property claims. And so we need to be set up for the big event. Um, we can handle these attritional losses out of, uh, cash flow, but ultimately there's a crown guarantee if a big event happens, and we haven't put enough money to pay for that.

So, and that, that was my understanding. So in terms of a big event, I get that just the ongoing claims that happen. Yes. And so I, I was looking at those events as they were occurring. Yes. And going, actually, this is terrible, obviously for the people who are affected and especially with the loss of life. Yes. But it wasn't gonna have a big impact on what you are doing.

Yes. What level of event is going to, as it were, trigger the need to invoke the Crown guarantee, because the reserves on that? Yeah. Um, so if you look at Canterbury, for example, we funded that by, uh, about $6 million out of reserves, you know, the natural hazard fund and about $5 million of reinsurance and a couple hundred million from the Crown.

So it was largely self-funded. Um, if we look at a modeled event for Wellington 7.8 earthquake or hick, um, say $15 billion, uh, the way that would be funded is we've got $10 billion of reinsurance. Wow. Um, and we've got, uh, an excess of, um, starting 2.5 billion.

Um, so, so that goes up to sort of 12 and a half. So the Crown would be on the hook for above that. And they're also on the hook for the balance from two and a half billion back down to our current balance of the NDF, which is currently about 600 million as well. Right. So one of just maybe sort of think now in terms of that levy not being increased at present Yes.

Is that gonna have an impact on the way that, um, raki can actually get its reinsurance book filled? No. No, we don't think so. No. Right. Um, you know, we do have levy income of $900 million a year. It's, you know, not insignificant. Um, so there is a choice about how much reinsurance we take.

Um, we could take no reinsurance. Um, you know, that's always a choice, but that would, um, expose the crown unnecessarily. So it is trying to find an optimal sort of sweet spot there and, and make sure we get value for money with the reinsurance purchase. Mm-hmm. Okay. Um, Tina, how's it been going, getting that reinsurance book filled that, oh, Um, well, Tina, uh, been nice to see you, Deborah.

Uh, the, yeah, really, well, actually, last year we, it was a record amount for us to, um, secure $10.3 billion worth of reinsurance, and we were able to do that. So we went up by a billion, but for the same price that we paid the year before. So actually very good. Oh, last year.

Um, and, uh, I think, um, I know, uh, some of the other committee members have met with some of the reinsurers in, in London and New Zealand has, you know, we are 80 years old now as a scheme, and so we have this longstanding relationship with the capital markets and we, it's incredibly important that we protect that when we are the second riskiest country in the world, and when we've got the financial pressures that the country is experiencing at the moment.

So we do invest a lot in those relationships, but we also get a lot of positive feedback, um, about that. And I imagine today we're going to go on to talk about, um, you know, the rising risks of, of climate change and things. And I just, if I can start with an optimistic note, the, um, the global commentators say, look, if anyone can do it, New Zealand can actually, because you've got some really good foundations in place, a fantastic public private scheme, really smart people.

No one has all the answers, but you're only 5 million people. So if you guys can join yourselves up together and get going, anyone can, you know? Right. That's, yeah. So I just thought I'd start with that positive message because it can feel, you know, difficult and challenging day to day. Yeah. I'm just getting incredibly technical now just because I'm interested.

Um, um, cat bonds, are you still, do you still got some Cat bonds? Well, we've got one year left on our, um, on our existing catastrophe bond. And the market changes very much from year to year. Yeah. Uh, so at the moment the cap bond market is taking off the traditional market is, there's plenty of capacity to deploy.

And so every year we just look at it for how are we going to get the best value for money out of our, our spend because we are buying a big chunk, 10 billion, and we are buying at a very high layer, uh, where we can, you know, get some good, um, return on our investment there. Cool.

Alright. I've got more questions, Cameron. Okay. No, That's all right. We'll go to the, on Barbaras. Yeah. Um, good morning, and thank you. Yeah, I mean, a lot of the questions I was thinking about is particularly around the reinsurance, uh, you've, you've addressed and yeah, confirming that, that's basically the comments that I'd had from the three reinsurers that I made quite resale in the last year or so.

Um, one of the other elements that they said that strengthens their confidence in New Zealand is the science that we have in relation to this. Do you, um, my apologies and my apologies to the previous minister, um, do you basically support part of that as part of GNS as part of that geotechnical work?

Mean? What is your involvement in that? Because obviously that supports your ability to model as well as, you know, know the long-term sustainability of the fund. Well, that's what I look like from a public perspective. Mm-hmm. Yeah. It's a massive part of our role. That's, that's right. And it's actually, so we also keep in touch with other schemes like ours, catastrophe schemes around the world.

And it is the thing that distinguishes us from the 20 other schemes like us around the world. We specifically, we call it our resilience portfolio. So we, uh, we invest around 15 million a year in science and research and funding geo net. But, and we've been doing that for decades, it's really important to not just be the ambulance at the bottom of the cliff.

It's really important to be the fence at the top. So you can only be an effective fence at the top if you understand what the risk is continuing to invest in that science. We have amazing scientists in New Zealand, so flowing that data, modeling and science, our role as NHC, as we take all of that, turn it into actionable insights that we then share across the whole system.

So to policy makers like MFE and MB who are setting the building codes to the professions when it comes to guidelines or professional codes or standards or building materials, those sorts of things. Councils, we make a lot of submissions on land use planning, don't let people build in the wrong places.

Um, even developers now actually coming to us for advice. We also, um, will share it with homeowners, really importantly. So, um, every person in the system needs to understand what is happening with the risk and how it impacts them and what they can do to reduce the risk.

And there's actually quite a lot everyone can do. So for homeowners, that's about where do I live, uh, or where am I about to buy using our natural hazards portal that we have. Um, what can I do to strengthen my property, go down to the hardware shop, you know, maintain my retaining walls. And last year we were really pleased with the impact.

When I think about the impact that that work has, we survey people last year, 61% of people said they not only read our materials, they engage with them, and then they took action to actually go down to the runnings or might attend and to, and do some work. So for us, that's actually the things we get really excited about, is convincing people to take action.

Plus all of the other things we measure, like the number of councils that change their land use planning decisions as a result of our advice, the number of policy agencies that rely on our advice. So we do measure that impact because it is important. Um, and that's the way, as a society, we're going to grasp this problem.

It's a, you know, this is the problem of our generation, isn't it? And do you find that most councils, I mean, having spoken to some councils, part of the issue is, is the good advice around mainly use planning? Yeah. They have their own engineers and obviously external engineers that support 'em to say, don't build in these areas.

But the problem is, is if you've got a homeowner who wants those C views, who's got deep pockets mm-hmm. Who also has an another engineer that can provide a different, it's not worth it for the council to take them to the environment court, basically stop the building there. Do you think there are areas where, where us as policy makers could strengthen the system in order to prevent what is going to be more and more often, um, areas which will be more state or due to heavier range because of climate change?

I think, um, there are some certain ways, uh, sorry. First of all, can I say that I think I'm really proud of the way the team has worked with councils. We started off by making formal submissions, and now we've formed relationships, and now councils come to us proactively. So everything's better if you're doing it through relationship base rather than a big stick.

Um, however, it is hard for councils who are stuck in the middle. They know we are the right place to build builders, but they, they do get litigated. And there's a deep pockets thing that happens. And we saw that happen two decades ago in Christchurch. Um, marshlands was once upon a time just marshlands and then, you know, became houses, and then it became a red zone.

So, um, there's a really stark example that we have in our recent history. What I think the councils appreciate about NHC is they can say, but here's the scientific evidence to say that you shouldn't build there. And everybody looking at that can know, that will know that an environment court will take that seriously. I mean, if I could put something on my wishlist, it would be something along the lines of a, a law change that said, local councils must take account of the NHC scientific evidence that would probably help councils

that would actually, you know, help us. And it might minimize some of that litigation. I don't know, it would have to be studied, but just If you've got a lot of other law changes My one time this down. The only thing I would say, the other thing I would say is actually there's definitely a mind shift. Um, so if you look at the last five to 10 years, people are definitely, uh, more aware, um, and more understanding of natural power risk.

And so one of your points is, um, are people, uh, clear about who's responsible for what and who's gonna pay for what. So you can buy a house there or build a house there, but just be really clear of the implications around that. I think, you know, communication and really understanding who, who's dealing with what responsibility and who's paying last One. Yeah. Okay. Yeah. Um, sorry, I'm, last question.

Uh, we are seeing more risk-based pricing from retail insurance companies, um, in some jurisdictions across the world. They've gone to parametric pricing because of the fact that, um, basically they can't ensure for any other reasons. Do you think they'll have any implications on NHC cover in the future if they do change to a more pyramid sort of pricing system?

Uh, look, you have, um, yeah. The private sector has risk based pricing to your point. And that's actually helpful in a, in a way because it sends the message around where risk Yeah. Greater information education. No, no, that's right. And, uh, even though it's bring to high. So for us, we're a first loss scheme and we're a community rate scheme. So that's a, a design feature, um, of the act and, and what we have as, um, as a country, uh, this first loss, um, dynamic.

So, um, there's an interplay with, with the private sector, um, the question is where, where's the boundary on it? Um, and that could be around, um, you know, for us as the cat and also we have land cover. So look, we work well with the private sector. They're complimentary, but they're quite different schemes. Okay. Oh, sorry. Should it be possible just to, again, to, um, just to add to what Chris has said as well, I think it affordability is such a life issue, and it is a fair issue for everyone to be thinking about.
